review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions about intrinsic versus extrinsic evaluation, analogy and similarity benchmarks, and NER as a downstream test. The distinctions are stated directly, so no separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What best distinguishes intrinsic evaluation from extrinsic evaluation?
choices: Intrinsic uses unlabeled data, while extrinsic uses labeled data only
Intrinsic scores an internal subtask directly, while extrinsic tests impact on a downstream task
Intrinsic always measures accuracy, while extrinsic always measures F1
Intrinsic applies only to language models, while extrinsic applies only to classifiers
question_bank.explanation: Intrinsic evaluation measures a component on a focused internal test, while extrinsic evaluation asks whether that component helps a real end task.

question_bank.question: What does a word-vector analogy benchmark typically measure?
choices: How fast vectors can be loaded into memory
The percentage of analogy questions solved correctly by vector arithmetic
The number of words in the vocabulary
Whether every word has an antonym
question_bank.explanation: Analogy evaluation checks how often vector arithmetic retrieves the intended answer on a held-out set of relational word problems.

question_bank.question: Why is named entity recognition an example of extrinsic evaluation for word vectors?
choices: Because it directly asks humans to score similarity
Because it tests whether embeddings improve performance on a real downstream labeling task
Because it uses only count-based vectors
Because it does not require any labels
question_bank.explanation: NER is a full downstream task. If adding word vectors improves the system, that is evidence the embeddings are useful beyond isolated probes.
